coredata.dc:title: An optical nanosensor fabricated by carbon dots embedded in silica molecularly imprinted polymer for sensitive detection of ceftazidime antibiotic
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Amiri, Amir
Faridbod, Farnoush
Zoughi, Sheida
coredata.dc:description: Ceftazidime (CF) is an antibiotic, used in treatment of many serious bacterial infections. Determination of this antibiotic is essential because of its dangerous side effects. This work introduces a novel sensitive optical nanosensor based on carbon dots (CDs) synthesized using citric acid through bottom-up method, embedded in silica molecularly imprinted polymer (SMIP). This probe was made by embedding CDs as fluorophore materials, and ceftazidime as a template molecule in SMIP then washing the template molecules to provide CDs embedded in SMIP (CDs-SMIP). The synthesized materials were characterized by UV–vis (UV/Vis), fluorescence spectrophotometry, Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and Scanning electron microscope (SEM). The obtained CD-SMIP showed strong fluorescence emission with a symmetric peak at 420 nm using excitation at 340 nm. This synthesized probe was finally applied for the detection of ceftazidime. The fluorescence intensity of CDs-SMIP quenched in presence of ceftazidime molecules. The designed fluorescence probe responded to concentration of ceftazidime over a linear range of 0.18−1.27 μg.L−1 (0.33–2.32 nmol.L−1), also, the limit of detection (LOD) and the relative standard deviation (RSD) were 0.06 μg.L−1 (0.11 nmol.L−1) and 3.20% (n = 5), respectively. The introduced chemical nanosensor was used to analyze ceftazidime in urine samples satisfactory.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ceftazidime
Fluorescence quenching
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Nanosensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and preparation of CDs and CDs-SMIP
Preparation of the fluorescent probe to detect ceftazidime
Synthesis and characterization of CDs and CDs-SMIP
Optimization of parameters of the sensor
Analytical performance of CDs-SMIP for determination of ceftazidime
Selectivity of the proposed optical nanosensor
Analysis of ceftazidime in urine sample
Comparison of the fabricated optical nanosensor with other methods reported for ceftazidime detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of CDs (A), CDs-APTES (B), and CDs-SMIP (C).
TEM images of CDs-SMIP (A) and TEM images of CDs-SNIP (B).
SEM images of CDs-SMIP (A) and SEM images of CDs-SNIP (B).

Fluorescence spectra of nanocomposites CDs-SMIP (A) and CDs-SNIP (B) in the presence of different concentrations of ceftazidime 0.18 to 1.27 μg.L−1; The linearity range and the sensitivity of using nanocomposite CDs-SMIP and CDs-SNIP to the detection of ceftazidime (C); The CDs-SMIP and CDs-SNIP dispersed in water and ethanol, the incubation time of 10 min, pH of 7, and concentration of CDs-SMIP and CDs-SNIP was 0.03 mg.L-1.

Selectivity of the CDs-SMIP during determination of similar compounds to ceftazidime (0.18 mg.L−1); The concentration of the CDs-SMIP was 0.03 mg.L−1; F0 and F as Fluorescent intensity were recorded before and after adding the compounds.
Preparation and application of CDs-SMIP for the detection of ceftazidime.
Determination of ceftazidime in spiked urine samples.
Comparison of the presented method with other reported methods for the determination of ceftazidime.

row.entry: Cyclic voltammetry using a carbon nanotube modified electrode
Cyclic voltammetry using platinum modified electrode
spectrophotometric based on the plasmon absorbance of silver nanoparticles

head.ce:title: An optical nanosensor fabricated by carbon dots embedded in silica molecularly imprinted polymer for sensitive detection of ceftazidime antibiotic

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Chemistry Department, School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical Chemistry Department, School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ceftazidime (CF) is an antibiotic, used in treatment of many serious bacterial infections. Determination of this antibiotic is essential because of its dangerous side effects. This work introduces a novel sensitive optical nanosensor based on carbon dots (CDs) synthesized using citric acid through bottom-up method, embedded in silica molecularly imprinted polymer (SMIP). This probe was made by embedding CDs as fluorophore materials, and ceftazidime as a template molecule in SMIP then washing the template molecules to provide CDs embedded in SMIP (CDs-SMIP). The synthesized materials were characterized by UV–vis (UV/Vis), fluorescence spectrophotometry, Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and Scanning electron microscope (SEM). The obtained CD-SMIP showed strong fluorescence emission with a symmetric peak at 420 nm using excitation at 340 nm. This synthesized probe was finally applied for the detection of ceftazidime. The fluorescence intensity of CDs-SMIP quenched in presence of ceftazidime molecules. The designed fluorescence probe responded to concentration of ceftazidime over a linear range of 0.18−1.27 μg.L−1 (0.33–2.32 nmol.L−1), also, the limit of detection (LOD) and the relative standard deviation (RSD) were 0.06 μg.L−1 (0.11 nmol.L−1) and 3.20% (n = 5), respectively. The introduced chemical nanosensor was used to analyze ceftazidime in urine samples satisfactory.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) semiconductor nanoparticles are photostable fluorophores with unique luminescent capacity dependent on their particles size, which have been used for many investigations in recent years [1,2]. QDs show higher photoluminescence (PL) quantum efficiency than other bulk materials [3]. Carbon dots (CDs) as a fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial, are an acceptable choice between the other QDs and they have attracted a great deal of attention because of their excellent properties over previous QDs and organic fluorescent particles [4]. These properties are biocompatibility, high fluorescence emission, narrow emission band, low toxicity and stable fluorescence intensity [5–7]. The main reason which limits application of CDs is related to suffering from low selectivity, so we can modify them with appropriate methods like molecular imprinting [8].

Molecular imprinting is a well-known technique for preparation of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) based on polymerization around target molecules [9]. The target molecules are then extracted and recognition sites are left behind. Molecular imprinting can be used to provide efficient polymers, including specific bioactive recognition sites with a memory of functional groups, shape and size of the target molecule [10]. The selective detection ability of a molecularly imprinted sensor is not only dependent on the amount of effectively created imprinted sites but also on the properties of nanomaterials [9]. Metal quantum dots, which were more common for use as a core of polymerization are limited now because of their toxicity [11]. Today, silica is used because silica materials have unique structural properties for imprinting matrices, as their structures are highly appropriate for the formation of a delicate recognition site [12]. Up to now, silica molecularly imprinted polymers (SMIPs) have been made on the surface of QDs, such as carbon dots (CDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). This method is a low cost and simple way in which eco-friendly solvents like water and ethanol can be used [13]. In this modification method, CDs are embedded in MIP, which provides modified nanoparticles named CDs-embedded MIP (CDs-MIP), which shows perfect selectivity and notable fluorescence emission. As a result, the expansion of the optical nanosensor based on the CDs-MIP has gained fantastic attention to detect target molecules [14].

Ceftazidime, as one of the third generation of cephalosporin, is used to treat bacterial infections caused by gram-negative bacteria and inhibit other pathogenic microorganisms [15,16]. Ceftazidime has a brilliant activity against bacteria in gram-negative group, which has made it an attractive choice for targeted therapy and empiric therapy [17]. Ceftazidime metabolites have not been identified and kidneys are mostly responsible for the elimination of ceftazidime into the urine. As such, drug levels in the blood may build up in persons with kidney injury or kidney disease and drug resistance [18]. Aggregation of this antibiotic in a body leads to significant allergic reactions, including photodermatitis and anaphylaxis, increasing eosinophils, lactate dehydrogenase, gamma-glutamyl transferase in blood, and gastrointestinal symptoms that can be harmful to the health of the human body [19,20]. Therefore, determination of ceftazidime is of particular importance. Currently, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [21], ultra-performance liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (UPLC-Mass) [22], infrared spectroscopy (IR) [23], and HPLC with UV detection (HPLC-UV) [24] are the most common determination methods for ceftazidime because of sensitivity and identification ability. However, these methods are suffering from long operation time, high cost, and multistep sample preparation. Thus, reporting a simple, rapid, and efficient method to detect ceftazidime in biological and environmental samples is crucial.
In this paper, a novel highly sensitive optical nanosensor based on CDs-SMIP was first time presented for selective, sensitive, rapid, and simple detection of ceftazidime.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), citric acid, ethanol, and other reagents were bought from Merck Chemical Co. (Germany). Ceftazidime, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), and other substances were purchased from a regional company (Alborz, Tehran, Iran). The ceftazidime stock solution as a standard solution (10−7 mol.L-1) was made monthly in deionized water and stored at 25 °C. Working standard solutions of different concentrations were prepared daily by diluting the intermediate standard solutions with mobile phase solutions.

FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer Fourier transform infrared spectrometer using KBr pellet technique. TEM images and SEM images were obtained using an EM900 (Zeiss) and a Hitachi S4160 electron microscope, respectively. Fluorescence measurements were done by a Perkin-Elmer LS50 luminescence spectrometer. UV/Vis spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer (Lambda 25) UV/Vis spectrometer. Also, a Milli-Q system (Millipore Corp.) to purify required water for solution preparation, LASER (USA), Ultrasonic Cleaner for sonicating, VELP, SCIENTIFICA for stirring, a centrifuge device made by Germany Heidoiph Company for centrifuging and, a Micronal B474 pH meter equipped with an Ag/AgCl/KCl (3.0 mol L−1) glass combined electrode for recording the pH values, were used.

Kidneys largely eliminate ceftazidime therefore in this proposed work, urine was selected to use as real sample for ceftazidime detection. Urine sample was collected from a healthy case. It was filtered and diluted by distilled water 100 times. Then a certain amount of ceftazidime was added and analyzed by the fabricated nanosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and preparation of CDs and CDs-SMIP
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were prepared by modifying previously reported methods [25,26]. In this method, citric acid (2.00 g) was heated at 180 °C. Changing the color of molten from colorless to pale yellow is indicative of reaction completion. The synthesized product was CD nanoparticles, which were used for CDs-SMIP synthesis. For this purpose, 8 mL of APTES was added to CDs while was stirring, and heated at 35 °C for 30 min. The solution was then cooled to 25 °C afterward. Then, 0.05 mg of ceftazidime as a template molecule was dissolved in 2 mL anhydrous ethanol, which was added to 1.50 mL of APTES-CDs. Then 1.20 mL TEOS and 0.30 mL of a catalysis solution, NH4F/NH4OH (1.80 g NH4F, 80 mL H2O, and 20 mL NH4OH), was added to the mixture and stirred for 10 min more. Finally, the template was removed through washing by a solution of ethanol and water (2:5) several times. Also, a nanocomposite based on CDs embedded in silica non-imprinted polymer (CDs-SNIP) was synthesized as control material using the same method, without adding ceftazidime as template. The fluorescence intensity of the washed CDs-SMIP quenched in presence of ceftazidime as an analyte (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent probe to detect ceftazidime
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the fluorescent probe, 1 mg of CDs-SMIP powder was added to a 100 mL volumetric flask containing a phosphate-buffered solution (PBS), and the solution was sonicated for 4 h. For each measurement, 10 μL of the sonicated was added into a 4.5 mL-fluorescent quartz cuvette containing 3 mL PBS. The dispersed CDs-SMIP solution was stored at 4 °C for further measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs and CDs-SMIP

ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CDs was confirmed by UV/Vis spectrum with a broad band from 300 to 360 nm due to л-system, the bandgap and absorption edge of CDs (Fig. S2B). The excitation wavelength of CDs-SMIP was determined using UV/Vis spectrum and opted at 340 nm. Fluorescence emission spectra of synthesized CDs-SMIP showed an emission peak at 420 nm excited at 340 nm. Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-SNIP, CDs-SMIP before extraction, and CDs-SMIP after extraction of ceftazidime indicated that the fluorescence of CDs-SMIP increases next to removing the template molecules, which corresponds to the disappearing of electron transfer between CDs and ceftazidime molecules (Fig. S2A).

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of the CDs-SMIP was extensively studied. Based on IR spectra, hydrogen bonds' formation is probable between ceftazidime and the NH2 − groups of APTES. This may explain an electron transfer mechanism for fluorescence quenching in which electrons are transferring between the highest occupied bands of the CDs to the lowest empty molecular orbital of ceftazidime. Furthermore, the spectra related to the absorption of ceftazidime and fluorescence emission of CDs-SMIP were not concurrent. Since there is no overlap between these two spectra, the energy transfer mechanism is not an appropriate mechanism for fluorescence quenching [27], so the analyte can be a receptor as CDs are fluorophores and the mechanism of quenching is electron transfer [28].

The Stern-Volmer equation can be used to quantify the fluorescence quenching of CDs-SMIP [29]:
(1) F0/F = 1 + Ksv [Q]
In Eq. (1), F shows the fluorescence intensity of CDs-SMIP after adding the quencher and F0 is defined as the intensity of fluorescence before adding the quencher. Ksv is a constant related to quenching, and [Q] shows the quencher concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters of the sensor

ce:section.ce:para: To find an appropriate working pH of the measurements, the pH effect of the buffer solution on the response of the optical nanosensor was investigated. To achieve this purpose, CDs-SMIP was prepared in PBS in a pH range of 5–9. The best quenching response of CDs-SMIP in the presence of ceftazidime (0.20 μg.L−1) was obtained at pH 7 (Fig. S3A). The intensity of the response decreased by increasing pH, most probably due to the ionization of the silica layer under highly alkaline condition which can cause damage to the structure of the binding sites [30]. In addition, the sensitivity decreased when pH was lower than 7 because the surface of the composites may be dissolved leading to surface defects and protonation of the amine groups in CDs-SMIP structure occurred, which decreases bonding ability between the active sites and the ceftazidime [31]. Based on these justifications, the appropriate pH for detection of ceftazidime was selected 7.

The time for maximum adsorption of ceftazidime on CDs-SMIP was optimized. Incubation time while the other effective parameters such as pH [7], temperature (25 ± 1 °C), and ceftazidime concentration (0.20 μg.L−1) were considered constant, was studied. First, ceftazidime was added to CDs-SMIP, and then the fluorescence intensity of the mixture after 0, 5, 10, and 15 min was recorded. 10 min after addition of ceftazidime, a maximum decrease in emission was observed. Then, it was almost constant during incubation time more than 10 min (Fig. S3B). Therefore, the suitable adsorption time for the detection of ceftazidime was 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of CDs-SMIP for determination of ceftazidime
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of the designed optical nanosensor was studied in the optimized conditions. The fluorescence spectra of CDs-SMIP and CDs-SNIP in the existence of different amounts of ceftazidime were recorded (Fig. 5 A and B). The synthesized CDs-SMIP and CDs-SNIP showed an appropriate linear range from 0.18 μg.L−1 to 1.27 μg.L-1 (Fig. 5C). The determination coefficient (R2) was 0.994 and 0.986 for CDs-SMIP and CDs-NIP, respectively. The LOD (3SDblank/slope) for detection of ceftazidime was 0.06 μg.L−1. This very low LOD proved that the proposed optical nanosensor was highly applicable and sensitive in determining trace ceftazidime. The fluorescence measurements for various concentrations of ceftazidime were repeated five times and the relative standard deviation was 3.20 % (for 5 replicate measurements).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the proposed optical nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To show the selectivity, this optical nanosensor was investigated to detect ceftazidime and other components with similar chemical structure to ceftazidime. The results indicated a spectacular difference in response to ceftazidime compare to other similar components (Fig. 6 ). During the synthesis of CDs-SMIP, many cavities and recognition sites were created that were in the same size and shape related to ceftazidime structure; therefore, this optical nanosensor based on CDs-SMIP had an excellent selectivity to ceftazidime.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ceftazidime in urine sample
ce:section.ce:para: To study the applicability of the proposed optical nanosensor in real matrices, the optical nanosensor based on CDs-SMIP was used to detect ceftazidime in urine samples. The CDs-SMIP fluorescent probe exhibited excellent quenching in response to different concentrations of ceftazidime in some spiked urine samples. In this analysis, concentrations of spiked ceftazidime were 0.40, 0.80, 1.20, and 1.60 μg.L−1. The recovery of ceftazidime from spiked urine matrix was 97.50–107.50 % (n = 5, average of five replicate measurements) (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the fabricated optical nanosensor with other methods reported for ceftazidime detection
ce:section.ce:para: This work was compared to some other reported methods which have tried to detect ceftazidime (Table 2 ). Table 2 is evidence to indicate the efficiency of the proposed method, which shows the detection limit and linear range of the different techniques for the determination of ceftazidime. Based on Table 2, our proposed nanosensor showed an appropriate detection limit and linear rang compared to reported works up to now. This method can be considered successful because of some remarkable features such as low detection limit, appropriate linear range, low cost, and biocompatibility.

An optical nanosensor based on CDs-SMIP was fabricated to detect ceftazidime. The CDs-SMIP was prepared using the reaction of APTES and TEOS with CD nanoparticles in the presence of the template molecules. This fluorescent probe consisted of the brilliant properties of SMIP and CDs such as high specificity and strong fluorescence emission, which produced for the first time an optical nanosensor to highly sensitive and selective determination of ceftazidime at trace levels. This optical nanosensor is a simple, low cost, rapid, reliable, and eco-friendly method for the quantification of ceftazidime in biological samples. Also, other fluorescent nanosensors can be prepared using this excellent probe for organic compounds monitoring and different real-life samples. In this work, we tried to detect ceftazidime in biological samples with a detection limit of 0.06 μg.L−1.
We the undersigned declare that this manuscript is original, has not been published before and is not currently being considered for publication elsewhere.

We confirm that the manuscript has been read and approved by all named authors and that there are no other persons who satisfied the criteria for authorship but are not listed. We further confirm that the order of authors listed in the manuscript has been approved by all of us.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the University of Tehran.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.113111.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
